data.title: TLT: High Term Premium And Low Credit Spreads Make Long Bonds A Relative Bargain
data.description:
In my last article on the iShares 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF (TLT), I highlighted that high yields on long-term Treasuries reflected the high term premium rather than expectations of higher rates over the long term, which provided an opportunity in the ETF. This thesis remains intact, and considering the extremely low credit spreads on corporate bonds, the TLT is poised to significantly outperform.

The TLT ETF

The TLT has a maturity of 26 years and a duration of 15.5 years, making it highly susceptible to long-term rate expectations. The current average yield to maturity of the fund's assets is 4.9%, which compares with long-term inflation expectations of 2.3%, so investors should expect to receive 2.6% annual returns in real terms. The reason falling long-term rate expectations have failed to bring yields down over recent years is because the term premium has more than offset this impact. This has resulted in the ETF now offering a higher yield than many corporate bond funds, despite the significantly higher liquidity and lower default risk of Treasuries.

Where Bond Returns Come From

The yield on long-term bonds can be broken down into expectations of where short-term rates will average over the long term, plus a premium to compensate for higher duration. Based on the Adrian, Crump, and Moench 10-year estimate and the Bloomberg estimate of the UK 30-year term premium, we can infer that the US 30-year term premium is around 2%. This means that over the next 30 years, long-term Treasuries are expected to outperform cash by at least 2% annually. This is very high from a historical perspective and has been justified by the US' rising debt load, which has created uncertainty over the government's ability to repay its debt over the long term.

Relative Value

However, the government can always rely on the Federal Reserve to buy its bonds in the case that lack of private demand threatens a debt default. If policymakers were to allow a default, then it would likely blow up the entire global debt market and cause huge default rates across the corporate and other debt markets.

It is surprising then that these credit markets offer such low yields. As shown below, 30-year Treasuries now yield significantly more than short-term high-grade US corporates and are on par with mid-term high-grade US corporates.

Meanwhile, the spread of long-term high-grade corporates over long-term Treasuries has fallen to its lowest level since the late 1990s at just 80bps. This is around half of the long-term average and a full standard deviation below it.

A Bet Against Complacency

It is tough to envisage a scenario where long-term Treasuries underperform mid-term corporates for an extended period from current levels. Corporate bonds are subject to default risk and liquidity risk and are subject to state taxes, unlike Treasuries, so their yields should be naturally higher. Currently low credit spreads reflect high levels of complacency about the state of the economy, which has allowed investors to focus on the upside of marginally higher returns in a positive economic scenario rather than liquidity and default risk.

However, in the event of a shift in market sentiment, Treasuries could outperform significantly as investors decide current wafer-thin spreads are not worth the risk, causing a rush into the safety of Treasuries. Long-term Treasuries outperformed long-term investment-grade corporates by almost 50% during the global financial crisis and 70% during the Covid crisis. While another such risk event does not appear to be on the horizon, they rarely appear to be until yield spreads have already blown out and Treasuries have already outperformed significantly.

Summary

The TLT ETF is now extremely attractive relative to corporate bonds. With a term premium estimated to be around 2%, investors should now expect to see 2% annual outperformance relative to cash and 2.6% annual real returns after inflation. This is now higher than the expected return on many corporate bond funds despite the much higher liquidity and asymmetric upside risks in the event of a deterioration in risk appetite.

data.title: STMicroelectronics: Turning The Corner As Fundamentals Improve (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Today, we are analyzing STMicroelectronics (STM). Here at the Lab, we view the setup as constructive, even given STM's underperformance over the past 12 months (Fig. 1), which lagged peers such as Microchip (+30%) and Infineon (+28%). In our view, Microchip's recent preannouncements, particularly relevant given its industrial MCU positioning, point to improving near-term conditions that should also be supportive of STM. This is not the first time that we have covered STMicroelectronics, and our neutral stance was backed by multiple factors, including a pricey valuation, Depressed Earnings Ahead, and weak automotive demand. Additionally, we reported our note titled 3 Risks To Price, highlighting negative FX, Mobileye Global's inventory build-up, and Apple concentration. Our assessment of several of the risks outlined above has evolved, which makes this an appropriate time to reassess and update our forecast.

Fig. 1

Why Are We Positive?

Heading into Q4 2025 and looking at 2026, we see AI-led demand continuing to underpin sales growth. Thanks to our cross-read on Infineon, we see an Underappreciated AI Opportunity in STM as well. Additionally, the analog segment shows early signs of a cyclical upswing. Analog products, MEMS, and Sensors (AM&S) account for 45% of STM's current sales (Fig. 2). Against this backdrop, we prefer to focus on names that have lagged despite improving fundamentals. ASML and STM fit this profile and remain our top picks. Additionally, the EU semi-cap names have also materially underperformed their US counterparts. We might see favour capital recycling activities within the sector.

Fig. 2

The company guides Q4 top-line sales up 2.9%, below the usual ~7% seasonal uplift. This reflects the digestion of industrial channel inventory and ongoing automotive destocking. That said, taking advantage of our cross-coverage within the auto industry, we reported how "lobbying efforts by the European auto industry for more flexible post-2030 CO₂ targets." In our view, there was "a strong chance that the long-term CO₂ framework for carmakers will be eased," and this is what has happened. STM's main clients are in the Automotive segment (Fig. 2), which supports sales and margins.

In a previous analysis, we reported Apple and Mobileye Global client concentration risks. But Q2 2026 should follow typical seasonality; however, the company implied a +16% yearly growth. This will likely support margin expansion via operating leverage. We believe Apple launches will translate into higher volumes. This will lead to gradual improvement in both top-line sales and gross margin through 2026. We also see meaningful company-specific tailwinds next year, most notably Tesla and Mobileye, where easier comps could translate into differentiated growth.

Looking at the Q3 release, STM operating expenses were the main negative, with Q4 OPEX guided at ~$915 million, up from the ~$840 million recorded in Q3 (Fig. 3). We need to report that management has introduced cost actions. With easier FX, we expect some benefits in 2026. Even if we anticipate flat OPEX year over year, versus prior consensus expectations for a low- to mid-single-digit decline, we believe the company will be back to profitable growth. Here at the Lab, we read the expected ~180 basis points improvement in Q4 gross margin as encouraging (Fig. 4). A solid balance sheet and stable inventory level also support this.

Fig. 3

Fig. 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

In our last update (August 2025), STM valuation was expensive, with a P/E ratio above 30x. Since then, the company's EPS increased considerably on a quarterly basis (Fig. 5), and even if it still lags behind 2023/2024 results, we see light at the end of the tunnel. The company's market cap is €21 billion with a favorable net financial position of €2.6 billion. Additionally, to optimise STM strategic priorities, the company reduced its CAPEX investment, and we believe this will also be the 2026 trend. Looking toward 2026, we see a higher gross margin at 35.7%. This is an improvement over the 33.8% estimate in 2025. Even accounting for a slight negative FX, we see positive impacts from manufacturing efficiency, cost-saving initiatives, and the optimisation of unused capacity. Rolling forward our estimates, we forecast sales at €13.6 billion. This automotive positive momentum will persist into 2026, reinforcing our optimistic view on the analog complex. Going down to the P&L, we see EBIT at the 10% level. This is still well below the 2023-2024 numbers, but given lower CAPEX investment and a favorable financial position, we project net income of €1.2 billion. In our calculation, we used a 16% tax rate (aligned with the company's previous tax rate and included a favorable interest rate). With 911 million outstanding shares, STM's EPS is set at €1.31. This implied a P/E of 17.5x and an EV/EBITDA of 6x. Applying a 20x P/E (Fig. 6), aligned with STM's historical average, we derive a target price of €26.2 per share ($30.5 in ADR). We have not adjusted our valuation for the current cash and cash equivalents, and we also report a valuation cap compared to peers (which we believe is justified). That said, we moved our rating from neutral to buy.

Fig. 5

Fig. 6

Risks

Key downside risks include a larger-than-expected unused industrial capacity. The company is progressing with restructuring actions similar to those seen in Q1-Q2 2025. We believe additional negative one-offs will be taken negatively by Wall Street. Here at the Lab, despite a more benign view on Auto, we still see a challenging environment. We need to report a weaker backdrop in consumer electronics (Apple). Slower innovation and CAPEX coming above expectations are downsides to consider. Here at the Lab, we are closely monitoring inventory trends, and in the near term, STM pricing could remain under pressure. We also report a weakening of the $/€ exchange rate, and the medium-term risk centres on STM's ability to deliver new image-sensing products for smartphones.

Conclusion

Here at the Lab, we have consistently reported downside risks from STM. At this stage, the company will enter a favorable period due to easier comps and a positive underlying sector that is already covered. With a valuation still at a discount to peers, this might be a positive momentum to step in.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: United Parcel Service's Turnaround Story: Lower Expectations Doesn't Mean The Plan Is Working
data.description:
The idea of value on Wall Street is heavily rooted in what the market views as reasonable expectations. Oftentimes, one of the best ways to find a company that is undervalued or overvalued is to see when sentiment reaches uniquely high or low levels, because that frequently means that expectations are out of line with reality.

There are a few companies that have struggled with estimates over the last three years more than the United Postal Service (UPS). While the leading carrier saw record-high revenues and earnings during the pandemic, the company has consistently struggled to drive substantive revenue and earnings growth since early 2022.

UPS has offered investors total returns of negative 17.38 percent over the last 5 years, while the S&P 500 (SPY) has offered investors total returns of 97.27 percent during the same time period.

I last wrote about the United Postal Service in August of 2025, and I reiterated my strong sell rating after first rating this company a sell in April of 2023. Today, I am again reiterating my strong sell rating of the carrier. While UPS recently beat very low third-quarter expectations, the company's turnaround plan is still not working. The carrier continues to struggle with compressed margins, historically high capital expenditures, and stagnant revenue and earnings growth. UPS still looks overvalued at current levels.

Analysts had lowered expectations for UPS's recent earnings significantly, primarily because of the company's continued problems as well as the deteriorating economic outlook going into the report. The carrier reported third quarter earnings per share normalized actual of $1.74 and revenue actual of $21.42 billion, beating consensus analyst expectations of $1.55 and nearly $20.9 billion. The company guided to nearly $3.5 billion in capital expenditures this year, a consolidated operating margin of just over 13 percent. UPS has previously stated that the company expects consolidated revenues in 2026 to come in between $108 billion and $114 billion. The company is also projected to spend nearly $3.5 billion on capital expenditures as part of a 3-year plan previously announced in 2024. Management stated that the non-GAAP adjusted operating margin should be approximately 11.0% - 11.5%.

The most important metrics for UPS are shipping volumes and revenue per parcel. The core part of management's turnaround strategy is focused on cost-cutting and shifting to more profitable shipping to improve margins. UPS gets nearly 67 percent of the company's revenues from domestic shipping, and the carrier remains heavily dependent on the US market.

This is why investors should be concerned about the company's continued inability to drive any substantive revenue or earnings growth, even though margins improved slightly on a year-to-year basis in the recent quarter.

The carrier actually saw revenues and earnings per share fall on a year-to-year basis, even though margins were slightly higher. Revenue fell by 2.6 percent on a year-to-year basis, and EPS fell by 2.5 percent. The company also saw volumes fall. While the drop in volumes was predicted as UPS shifts away from Amazon and lower margin parcels, the company has now shown an ability to drive substantive earnings or revenue growth by focusing more on higher margin shipments. UPS saw volumes fall by nearly 11 percent on a year-to-year basis, while revenue per parcel rose by nearly 9.8 percent. While the carrier beat expectations primarily because consumer spending rose at a higher rate than expected on a macro level, there remains no clear evidence that the company's turnaround plan will work.

UPS's revenue and earnings growth have stagnated since late 2022, and the company's quarterly growth rate continues to be negative as well.

The carrier's overall net margins remain under pressure as well.

The company's net margin in the third quarter of last year was just 6.15 percent.

While UPS has remained committed and able to pay current annual dividend payments, with the company paying $5.5 billion in dividends in 2025, up from $5.4 billion in 2024, the current $5 billion share buyback plan authorized in 2023 remains lackluster. UPS is on target to buyback just $1 billion in shares a year, even though the company has seen the market cap collapse over the last 3 years. Management's reluctance to even moderately increase the buyback plan or shift some of the dividend payments to share buybacks should signal a lack of confidence in the turnaround plan to investors, given how severely shares have sold off since 2022.

This is also why UPS looks overvalued at the current valuation level. The carrier currently trades at 16.36x expected forward earnings. Even though UPS's 5-year average valuation is 15.93x forecasted forward earnings, the company faces much harder comparisons now since record revenues were hit during the pandemic.

UPS is expected to grow earnings at just 7-9 percent over the next 5 years, and these forecasts also seem optimistic since the company has still not shown the ability to drive substantive revenue or earnings growth with the current plan management has struggled to implement over the last several years. The middle class is also struggling with record-high credit card debt and high prices. The recent rise in consumer spending, mostly by higher-income earners, is not likely sustainable.

All investment theses have risks, and UPS's recent rise in capital expenditure levels was part of a 3-year plan announced in 2024 that is expected to end this year. The company's current expectations are to spend nearly $3.5 billion in 2026, but management may cut spending levels significantly moving forward, since UPS also plans to continue to cut costs. The carrier eliminated 48,000 jobs in 2025 and was on track as of the third quarter of last year for nearly $3.5 billion in savings for all of last year. Management has shown an ability to reduce costs, even though these actions have not translated to the bottom line. UPS's cash flow also remains steady; the company should be able to continue to pay the dividend management has recently committed to.

UPS was one of the best-performing companies during the pandemic, but the carrier's earnings and revenue growth have stagnated over the last three years. Management has also spent aggressively during this time period and begun to implement a plan to shift to higher-margin parcels, but the fall in earnings shows the company is still not able to drive meaningful profitability with these initiatives. While UPS should continue to see solid cash flow and stable revenues, investors should be able to find better value elsewhere.

data.title: Sea Limited: Riding This Wave Higher; Bullish Opportunity
data.description:
Sea Limited (SE) has been on many investors' radars over the years, given its massive growth opportunity across developing markets. Often pegged as the "Amazon of Southeast Asia," the company has not only maintained its leadership position in its home market but has made an impressive expansion into other emerging markets.

The stock has been on a precipitous fall since October 2025, losing almost 40% of its value. A majority of this fall has occurred since the company's mixed Q3 report, which saw another strong period of revenue growth, but profitability that fell below expectations.

As discussed more below, the profit miss was disappointing, but this weakness should be more than priced into shares at this point. I believe Sea has turned into a contrarian, underappreciated large-cap stock that is poised to rebound in 2026.

The company has a history of very strong execution, despite frequent periods of market turbulence. Given its positioning in Southeast Asia and other emerging markets, there have been increased fears around potential tariffs. At this point, it is a guessing game as to which country President Trump will establish new tariffs against. Nevertheless, Sea's competitive positioning is strong, and the long-term health of the business is clear.

With the stock off to a rocky start in 2026, I believe this presents a very attractive buying opportunity. The upcoming quarter will be a big catalyst, as management will provide additional commentary on how they expect 2026 to play out. But given the stock's weakness, I believe there could be an asymmetric reward that skews higher.

Financial Review

At face value, Sea's Q3 report was very strong. As with almost every company, there are moving parts each quarter that should be monitored. For Sea, the big concern during Q3 was profitability, as discussed more below.

For the quarter, total revenue grew 38% yoy to $6.0 billion, which continues to be led by strong performance in E-commerce. Consolidated adjusted EBITDA was $874.3 million, which grew 68% yoy.

The company's largest revenue stream, E-Commerce, once again delivered an impressive quarter of growth. Gross orders and GMV both grew 28% yoy to 3.6 billion and $32.2 billion, respectively. Given the scale of this business, which now sits at a $4.3 billion quarterly run-rate, these growth rates are very healthy. One of the key drivers of the business has been increased focus on international growth. Shopee is the market leader in Southeast Asia, but there are significant greenfield opportunities in other geographies, most notably Latin America, which Sea is going after.

The trade-off with investing in growth opportunities is often some margin pressure. During the quarter, adjusted EBITDA margins for E-Commerce were 0.6% of GMV, which was actually down from 0.8-0.9% the past two quarters. Although this metric improved from the 0.1% margin in the year-ago quarter, the E-Commerce take-rate was 60 bps higher yoy. In a perfect world, the higher take-rate would almost 100% flow through to adjusted EBITDA margins, so the step-back in margin this past quarter caused stock weakness.

Management addressed this issue on the call.

If you look at a year-to-year basis, as we shared before, we will obviously see quarter-to-quarter fluctuation sometimes for seasonality reasons for some of the investment cycle of the initiatives, and it could also be a particular market status in terms of where we are pushing some of these initiatives. So I think if you look at a bit of a year-to-year trend, even going forward, I think we believe that we are able to deliver the 2% to 3% EBITDA margin.

In terms of where we are investing, one of the things we mentioned in the opening is further investment into the logistics and fulfillment capabilities. And beyond that, we are also deepening our buy engagement and wallet shares through, for example, our Shopee VIP program that we shared in the opening as well.

Most of our CapEx is just for the improvement of building the warehouses or sorting facilities, et cetera. For our buy engagement and wallet share program on Shopee VIP, it is also less fixed. Obviously, you will see a little bit of investment in the early days to get everybody understand the program and join the program. But as time goes on, it should be quite a profitable program, as you probably have seen in other e-commerce platforms across the globe.

In my opinion, sacrificing some margin is okay. Especially since the company has already shown its ability to scale profits in the E-Commerce business, taking the near-term hit to improve its logistics and fulfillment capabilities is a great investment. In fact, the company could eventually see more margin pressure as international expansion efforts take hold. Building out the infrastructure is not an easy task, and while Sea does not own the land, these are necessary investments to scale the business and establish market presence. I am in favor of this near-term margin dilemma.

Within the Digital Financial Services segment, revenue growth was 31% yoy to $653 million. The company's loan business related to Shopee remains very strong as they continue to further penetrate GMV opportunities. For example, loans principal outstanding reach $7.9 billion, with Thailand's loan book now >$2 billion and Brazil, a relatively newer market for Sea, growing >3x yoy.

Importantly, Sea is starting to see increased traction with off-Shopee loans. With >90% of loans still being originated through the Shopee platform, this demonstrates strong traction. However, there remains an opportunity with the off-Shopee loan portfolio, which saw >300% yoy growth during the quarter.

In my opinion, the most important metric for this portfolio is the non-performing loans, a key indicator for the underlying credit risk. During the quarter, this metric was 1.1% for loans >90 days, which is down slightly from the 1.2% seen in the year-ago quarter. This demonstrates that Sea is successful at not only scaling the business but also maintaining prudent credit controls on its loan portfolio, ultimately helping profitability.

Finally, the Digital Entertainment business continues its upward momentum. The company now has 670.8 million quarterly active users, with 9.8% being paid users—both representing record levels. This business segment is largely driven by the success of Free Fire, a mobile game with >100 million average daily active users. Strong performance was driven by two high-impact campaigns, Squid Game and Naruto Shippuden Chapter 2, both of which received positive user responses, per management.

Recent Positive Updates

Shortly after the negative stock reaction to the Q3 quarter, many analysts were out defending the business. Sure, the stock has been down even more since these positive updates were published; however, it is important that large Wall Street banks, such as Morgan Stanley, Jefferies, and Bank of America, are all reiterating their bullish thesis.

Even more recently, Sea announced a $1 billion share repurchase. Although a small amount relative to its >$70 billion market cap, this is a clear signal that the company believes its shares are undervalued. The company announced the share repurchase after the Q3 report, so it is likely Sea has been in the market repurchasing shares over the past few months.

Investors won't be able to see how many shares were repurchased until the next quarter, but I believe this is a very positive indicator. Particularly at a time when the stock weakness appears to be driven by concerns around profitability, I believe this is a signal that Sea is confident in the longer-term margin trajectory—as mentioned above—and believes they have additional flexibility to repurchase shares.

Valuation

Over the years, one of the biggest bear points on Sea was that the valuation was too expensive. With technology companies experiencing a valuation reset for much of last year, that argument is becoming more difficult to make.

As seen in the chart above, the company's forward P/E has now reset to just 24x, significantly below its peak of ~60x and approaching the all-time low.

If one were to just look at the pure financials of the company, with revenue growing 35%+ and a consolidated adjusted EBITDA margin of nearly 15%, I believe many would agree that the P/E multiple should be much higher.

Of course, there are nuances to this investment, such as material international exposure, an ongoing investment cycle pushing margins lower near-term, and geopolitical uncertainty.

However, I believe it will not take anything heroic to see the multiple stabilize and start to expand. The upcoming earnings report will be a great indicator of where management sees the revenue/margins going in 2026. Absent any significant negative surprises, I believe the risk-to-reward skews positive.

Risks

One of the biggest risks to my bullish thesis is that Sea ends up resetting margins lower to undergo an investment cycle. Building out infrastructure in emerging markets is not easy, but these are required investments in order to scale the E-Commerce business. Investors do not like stories where margins are being pushed lower, and this negative surprise could cap upside to the stock.

Additionally, competition is very high. Sea has developed a market-leading position in its home territory—Southeast Asia. Success in markets such as Latin America is not guaranteed. For example, MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI) is often viewed as the E-Commerce leader in that market, typically being referred to as the "Amazon of Latin America."

Finally, rising geopolitical tensions have already resulted in tariffs being implemented. With many uncertainties as to which country is next to be tariffed and at which rate, this causes some concern. If Sea's operating countries were to be slapped with high tariffs, this could pull down margins or negatively impact demand.

Final Thoughts

The 40% pullback in shares has been a culmination of several events. Partially driven by macro uncertainty and partially driven by concerns about profit margins (E-Commerce), investors have been avoiding this name.

However, valuation is now approaching all-time lows at the same time revenue growth remains 35%+. Sea has demonstrated its ability to scale margins, with consolidated adjusted EBITDA margins already near 15%. Although there could be a near-term reset in the business, I believe the company's platform and diverse business segments are deserving of a much higher valuation than the stock currently has.

With earnings just a few weeks away, I believe this pullback provides a great entry point for long-term investors. Staying bullish here despite the negative sentiment across the market.

data.title: Conestoga SMid Cap Composite Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
SMid Cap Composite Net Performance (as of 12/31/25)*

SMid Cap Composite – 4Q25 Performance & Attribution

The Conestoga SMid Cap Composite (CCSMX) trailed the Russell 2500 Growth Index in the fourth quarter with a total return of -2.71% net-of-fees versus the benchmark return of 0.33%. Relative performance was volatile throughout the quarter. A sharp market drawdown which saw the Index decline by more than -10% from October 27th through November 20th, provided the portfolio with the strong downside capture we’ve come to expect during such periods. The market rallied sharply however from November 20th through year-end and the portfolio gave back much of its relative gains. For the quarter, negative stock selection effects in the Industrials and Real Estate sectors were the primary drivers of underperformance, while an underweight to the highly volatile biotechnology industry also detracted from relative returns. These losses were partially offset by positive stock selection in the Financials and Basic Materials sectors. From a factor perspective, a "tug-of-war" between high-quality and low-quality factors persisted throughout the quarter, with unprofitable companies once again pulling away and outperforming their profitable counterparts, providing a significant headwind to relative performance.

Stock selection proved most challenging in the Industrials sector, with the biggest losses coming from Trex Co., Inc. (TREX) and Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD). Performance for TREX was pressured due to heightened competitive intensity, including increased pricing pressure and share competition. ROAD was one of the portfolio’s biggest winners through the first nine months of the year but gave back some of its gains in the fourth quarter after earnings results missed analyst estimates. These losses were partially mitigated by strong performance from Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY) and CSW Industrials, Inc. (CSW). Both companies benefited from a macro environment that rewarded essential, stable growth businesses with low sensitivity to economic and rate volatility, as investors favored visibility and durability over cyclical exposure late in the year.

Four of our five portfolio holdings in the Health Care sector were additive to relative returns with our high-conviction position in Repligen Corp. (RGEN) leading the way higher. Shares rallied in the fourth quarter as the broader bioprocessing market showed clear signs of recovery with the company delivering encouraging order growth. Stevanato Group SpA (STVN) was the only detractor in the sector. While demand for its high-value syringes and vials remained robust, the slower-than-expected recovery in its engineering segment dampened overall results. The biggest headwind for relative performance in the sector however was our lack of exposure to the biotechnology industry. After a prolonged period of valuation compression, the group rallied 21% during the quarter as stabilized interest-rate expectations improved sentiment toward long-duration growth sectors.

On the positive side, stock selection in the Financials, Basic Materials, and Utilities sectors provided a boost to relative results. Within Financials, Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc. (CWAN) surged following the announcement that the company would be acquired, which drove a re-rating toward the agreed transaction value. Both of our holdings in Basic Materials added value with RBC Bearings, Inc. (RBC) being the biggest winner. Shares rallied on strong quarterly results, driven by robust demand in the aerospace and industrial markets. Our sole position in Utilities, Casella Waste Systems, Inc. (CWST), added to relative returns during the quarter, particularly when stocks started selling off in late October.

SMid Cap Composite – Full Year 2025 Performance & Attribution

The Conestoga SMid Cap Composite trailed the Russell 2500 Growth Index for the full year 2025 with a total return of -4.71% net-of-fees versus the benchmark return of 10.31%. Almost all the underperformance occurred from the market bottom on April 8th through October 27th when the Index rallied 46%. Relative performance during this period was significantly impacted by a market environment that favored low-quality, high-beta, and unprofitable companies. Furthermore, the Index experienced remarkably narrow leadership, with a majority of the benchmark's gains concentrated in a small group of top contributors—most of which were concentrated in AI-related themes, hardware, and defense stocks.

For the annual period, negative stock selection effects in the Technology, Industrials, and Health Care sectors were the primary drivers of underperformance, while selection and allocation effects in Consumer Discretionary, Consumer Staples, and Financials sectors provided the most meaningful offsets. Stock selection proved most challenging in the Technology sector, where several long-term holdings faced valuation compression. SPS Commerce, Inc. (SPSC), Descartes Systems Group, Inc. (DSGX), and Tyler Technologies, Inc. (TYL) were the largest detractors within the sector. SPSC and DSGX both saw shares retreat as investors rotated out of software-as-a-service (SaaS) providers in favor of the hardware and AI-infrastructure names that dominated the Index returns. Similarly, TYL shares faced pressure as the market prioritized immediate AI-driven growth over steady, long-term cloud transitions.

Within the Industrials sector, Trex Co., Inc. (TREX) and Watsco, Inc. (WSO) also lagged. TREX struggled due to competitive pressures, while WSO shares were impacted by cooling demand for HVAC equipment following several years of record-breaking growth. While the benchmark's Industrials’ performance was bolstered by defense-related stocks, our lack of exposure to defense contractors weighed on relative results. Within Health Care, the biggest headwind for relative returns was our lack of exposure to the biotechnology industry, which rallied 33% for the year.

On the positive side, stock selection in the Consumer Discretionary sector provided a boost to relative returns. Rollins, Inc. (ROL) was the largest contributor to portfolio returns for the year as the company continued to demonstrate its defensive growth characteristics, posting consistent organic revenue gains and margin expansion. In addition, our large underweight to the Financials sector and lack of exposure to the Consumer Staples sector provided a relative boost as both areas significantly underperformed the benchmark.

SMid Cap Composite – 4Q25 Top 5 Leaders

Repligen Corp. (RGEN)

RGEN supplies bioprocessing equipment and consumables used in biologic drug manufacturing. Shares rebounded strongly as evidence mounted that industry destocking had bottomed and order trends were stabilizing. Improved visibility into 2026 growth, combined with disciplined cost controls, drove operating leverage expectations higher. The market re-rated the stock as investors looked past near- term volatility and focused on RGEN’s high-quality consumables mix, strong market positions, and long-term biologics growth tailwinds.

RBC Bearings, Inc. (RBC)

RBC designs and manufactures precision bearings and engineered components for aerospace, defense, and industrial markets. The stock outperformed in 4Q25 as aerospace demand remained resilient, defense orders accelerated, and operating leverage drove margin expansion. Investors reacted positively to strong free-cash-flow conversion and continued successful integration of prior acquisitions. RBC’s exposure to long-cycle aerospace programs and mission-critical components supported earnings durability.

Jack Henry & Associates, Inc. (JKHY)

JKHY constitutes a leading provider of technology solutions and payment processing services primarily for community banks and credit unions. The stock performed well after the company reported solid fiscal first-quarter results, characterized by steady top-line growth and successful contract wins for its modernization solutions. Despite industry consolidation headwinds, JKHY’s consistent sales momentum and ability to secure long-term client relationships highlight the resilience of its business model and its critical role in the financial services infrastructure.

CSW Industrials, Inc. (CSW)

CSW operates as a diversified industrial growth company with well-established brands in the HVAC, plumbing, and engineered building solutions markets. Despite reporting the first organic decline in twelve quarters in its key Contractor Solutions segment, the weak report was well understood given the challenging residential HVAC equipment sales market in 2025. We believe the stock's outperformance in the quarter was aided by an early December upgrade by J.P. Morgan as well as the company closing its second large acquisition this year (Motors & Armatures Parts (MARS Parts), which increased the company's exposure to HVAC repair so it’s less reliant on replacement of equipment).

Clearwater Analytics Holdings, Inc. (CWAN)

CWAN provides cloud-based investment accounting and analytics software to institutional investors and insurers. Shares significantly outperformed in 4Q25 following the announcement that the company would be acquired, which drove a re-rating toward the agreed transaction value. The acquisition highlighted the strategic value of CWAN’s highly recurring revenue model, strong client retention, and critical role within institutional investment operations.

SMid Cap Composite – 4Q25 Bottom 5 Laggards

FirstService Corp. (FSV)

FSV is a leading provider of essential property services to residential and commercial customers in North America. Shares detracted from performance after the company reported third-quarter revenues that fell short of consensus expectations, despite meeting earnings per share estimates. Investors expressed concern over softer organic growth and near-term headwinds in certain service lines. We continue to believe the company’s recurring revenue model and leadership position in fragmented markets remain intact for the long term.

Pool Corp. (POOL)

POOL is the largest distributor of swimming pool supplies, equipment, and related outdoor products in the United States. The shares lagged as demand remained pressured by a slower housing and discretionary spending environment. Reduced new pool construction and renovation weighed on volumes. The company’s maintenance business is holding up well but deflation in its tricolor chemical business is pressuring revenue. Despite these headwinds, POOL’s scale, distribution advantages, and strong cash generation continue to support its long-term business fundamentals.

Trex Co., Inc. (TREX)

TREX manufactures composite decking and railing products for residential outdoor living markets. Performance was pressured in 4Q25 due to heightened competitive intensity, including increased pricing pressure and share competition from both established and lower-priced alternatives, which weighed on volumes and margins. While the company maintains market leadership and a strong brand, the stock reflected investor concern around rising competition and the sustainability of near-term profitability in a more crowded marketplace. We sold the stock in 4Q25.

Stevanato Group SpA (STVN)

STVN is a global provider of high-performance drug containment and delivery solutions for the pharmaceutical industry. The stock underperformed during the quarter, pressured by two key factors. First, the company’s engineering segment reported a 20% decline in revenue. Although the segment represents only about 15% of total revenue, its turnaround has taken longer than anticipated, weighing on overall results. Second, investor concerns have grown around the potential impact of FDA approval and the introduction of oral GLP-1 therapies. Given STVN’s focus on injectable drug delivery, uncertainty regarding the eventual market share of oral alternatives within the broader GLP-1 category has created near-term uncertainty.

Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD)

ROAD is a vertically integrated civil infrastructure company constructing roadways and highways across the Sunbelt. The stock underperformed during the quarter following the release of fiscal fourth-quarter results, where earnings per share missed analyst estimates. While the company delivered better-than-expected revenue growth and highlighted a record backlog, the market focused on the bottom-line shortfall. We remain confident in the company’s ability to benefit from sustained infrastructure demand and its disciplined acquisition strategy.

Source: FactSet

SMid Cap Composite – 4Q25 Buys*

AAON, Inc. (AAON)

AAON designs and manufactures high-efficiency HVAC systems tailored for commercial and industrial applications. As a long-standing holding in our Small Cap strategy, the recent pullback in the stock has created an appealing entry point for our SMid cap strategy. AAON exhibits strong growth prospects, driven by its significant exposure to the data center market through its BASX division, as well as its robust competitive position in the semi-custom rooftop sector.

Karman Holdings, Inc. (KRMN)

KRMN is a defense technology company specializing in the design and manufacturing of highly engineered, mission-critical systems for a diverse set of existing and next-generation space, missiles, hypersonic, and defense programs. KRMN generates mid-teens or better organic revenue growth that is complimented by tuck-in M&A. They sell to over 70 customers across more than 100 defense programs. Over 90% of revenue is IP protected and nearly 90% of revenue is bought by their customers on a sole source basis.

SMid Cap Composite – 4Q25 Sells*

Generac Holdings, Inc. (GNRC)

GNRC manufactures power generation and energy storage equipment for residential, commercial, and industrial customers. The position was sold in 4Q as near-term fundamentals remain challenged by demand normalization, elevated channel inventories, and continued margin pressure, limiting visibility into a sustained earnings recovery. While long-term opportunities in energy resilience remain attractive, increased cyclicality and execution uncertainty reduce alignment with our quality and stability criteria, leading us to reallocate capital toward opportunities with clearer growth durability.

Trex Co., Inc. (TREX)

TREX manufactures composite decking and railing products for residential outdoor living markets. Performance was pressured in 4Q25 due to heightened competitive intensity, including increased pricing pressure and share competition from both established and lower-priced alternatives, which weighed on volumes and margins. While the company maintains market leadership and a strong brand, the stock reflected investor concern around rising competition and the sustainability of near-term profitability in a more crowded marketplace. We sold the stock in 4Q25.
